titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Mount Yuncui

change.item: Abschnitt Related Monasteries eingefügt (vgl. Mount Dazhai)
neue Gliederung des Abschnitts "Inscriptions" und Text eingefüllt
Abschnitt Related Monasteries geändert auf Verweis auf Mount Tianchi.
Änderungen und Kürzungen von LL eingearbeitet

figure.head: Heavenly Column Peak of Mount Yuncui; photograph taken in 2006
雲翠山天柱峰，2006年照片

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 雲翠山是魯中山脈內的另一個刻經點。它矗立於魯中南北方向的大寨谷東面， 是大寨谷東南山嶺的山峰之一。雲翠山位於平陰縣西南偏南27公里、聊城市東南偏南51.5公里、東平縣西北20公里處（見封二地圖）。山頂海拔高度約為420米，山腳海拔高度約為130米。 雲翠山摩崖上没有現代人工開鑿痕跡，山路止於頂峰下1公里左右的位置。
雲翠山的地理位置是東經116.2939度，北緯36.0631度，相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行436410，北行3991180。 石刻在雲翠山頂正下方，面向正西。雲翠山以東北東3.3公里處，是北向的大寨山石刻。
在雲翠山東南偏南方3.8公里處，可能是海檀寺舊址。不過，由於位於其間南北方向綿延的山嶺，海檀寺和雲翠山互不在對方遠眺視線所及之處。
在雲翠山西北偏北方4.7公里處，是二鼓山，二山相互可見。從大寨山摩崖石刻之處，可以看到雲翠山，卻看不到刊刻在山巔另一側的石刻（地圖，）。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 山頂天柱峰可以經由一條狹窄的小路到達。峰名由紅色大字書寫在約8米高的一方巨岩的正面（）。 巨岩之上是林木遮擋的高台，鄉民在戰亂和迫害中曾來此躲避。小石屋的牆壁仍殘留在高台之上，經由崖壁上隱蔽而陡峭的石階可以到達。
題刻在天柱峰西麓表面，距地面約2.5米（）。由於步行石階沿西崖而築，要到達摩崖石刻並不困難。
在東崖之上，還有一些明代（1368-1644）的文人題刻（)。

div.head: 釋名
div.p: 山名來歷不詳。
《東阿縣志》（道光版）云：
div.quote: 峰頂巨石高數十丈修廣里許，其形正方狀，如印而有紐，謂之“印峰”，亦曰“天柱”。《東阿縣志》1: 138。民國版《東阿縣志》81，從之。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 見天池山崇梵寺之相關部分。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 雲翠山佛名由一個五人僧團所刊刻。其中一個供養人，比丘僧太，也出現在二鼓山石刻的題跋中。第二個供養人姓名，惜已損毀，可能就是二鼓山石刻提到的僧道顒。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 根據書法風格分析，石刻大約完成於北齊時期（550-574）。然而，有研究者認為石刻與僧安道壹（活躍於562-579）有關，為其早期作品，因而斷代於公元564年前後。桐谷征一 51；賴非107。

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 雲翠山相關研究，主要見於2003年出版的《北朝摩崖刻經研究［續］》，多處提及雲翠山。張總 4, 33；桐谷征一51, 74, 78；賴非104, 107, 120, 126；田熊信之180, 188。在2006年出版的《北朝摩崖刻經研究［三］》中，楊浩和郝莉對此處石刻有不同的看法。楊浩、郝莉 174。

div.head: 銘文總數
div.p: 雲翠山有一個佛名（雲翠山1號）及一個題刻（雲翠山2號）。

div.head: 雲翠山1號
div.p: 雲翠山天柱峰西麓，刊刻有一個“大空王佛”佛名。緊靠題銘的左側部分，刻有一個半圓形碑首的石碑輪廓，該碑狀輪廓線與文字重疊， 但碑身没有任何紋飾和題字（及）。這一碑狀輪廓線的刊刻年代不詳。
有關佛名“大空王佛”的含義，見洪頂山佛名的相關說明。關於該佛名在山東省的分佈，見洪頂山8號的討論。

div.head: 雲翠山2號
div.p: 在佛名之右的摩崖表面刊刻題銘，至少有五列，刻在崖面上的題銘整體呈矩形，三邊以突出的巖稜為框。每列刻一僧名，僧名前皆冠以“比丘”（）。
由於所有的人名前都冠有“比丘”之稱謂，很可能是刊刻前一條佛名的一個僧團。賴非認為該僧團來自附近的崇梵寺，賴非 107。 他也同意桐谷征一的說法，認為該僧團與洪頂山、鐵山和尖山石刻的贊助人及書丹者僧安道壹早期作品的刊刻相關。賴非 126；桐谷征一74-75。

div.head: 刊刻技法
div.p: 刊刻技法主要為 “凵” 形刻法及飛白刻法。

figure.head: Inscription bearing rocky cliff at the west side of the summit of Mount Yuncui; photograph taken in 2004
雲翠山頂西側石刻摩崖，2004年照片

div.head: 書法風格
div.p: 雲翠山1號可資對照的寫法，見天池山相關介紹。2號的書法筆畫異常細瘦，且稜角分明。

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Yuncui 雲翠山 is located within the north-south Dazhai Valley 大寨谷 of Shandong’s central ranges. It is one peak of the ridge that flanks the entire southeast side of this valley. Mount Yuncui is situated 27 km south-southwest of the city of Ping­yin 平陰, 51.5 km south-southeast of Liaocheng 聊城, and 20 km northwest of Dong­ping 東平 (map in front endpaper). Its peak is at an altitude of about 420 m, its foot at an altitude of about 130 m above sea level. There has been no modern activity at Mount Yuncui. The road leading up to the mountain ends about one kilometer below the summit.

Geographically, Mount Yuncui is situated 116.2939° east and 36.0631° north WGS84. This equals 436410 E (easting) and 3991180 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM. At a distance of 3.3 km to the east-northeast lies the north-facing inscription of Mount Dazhai 大寨山. From the inscription site of Mount Dazhai, Mount Yuncui can be seen, but not its inscriptions, as they are located at the other side of the peak (map, fig. ).

The original site of Haitan Monastery 海檀寺 was located at a distance of 3.8 km to the south-southeast. In between the two sites, a long ridge runs from north to south, inhibiting a view from Mount Yuncui to Haitan Monastery.
Mount Ergu 二鼓山 lies 4.7 km to the north-northwest of Mount Yuncui. Both mountains are within each other’s line of sight.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p: The inscriptions were carved right below the summit, which is named Heavenly Column Peak 天柱峰, facing west. The summit can be reached by a narrow path. The name itself is written in large red characters on the foremost edge of a huge rock about eight meters high (fig. ). The tree-covered plateau on top of this rock was formerly used by the local farmers as a retreat in times of war and persecution. Walls of small stone houses are still extant on the plateau, which can only be accessed by means of a hidden, steep flight of stairs hewn into the rock.

The inscriptions were carved onto the face of the western cliff of Heavenly Column Peak, at a height of about two and a half meters above the ground (fig. ). Since the footpath also runs alongside the western cliff, they are easy to reach.
On the eastern cliff are some later inscriptions carved by literati of the Ming dynasty (1368-1644) (fig. ).

figure.head: Ming literati inscription on the eastern cliff of Heavenly Column Peak on Mount Yuncui; photograph taken in 2006
雲翠山天柱峰東崖上的明代文人題刻，2006年照片

div.head: Place Name
div.p: The origin of the mountain’s name is not clear. The Gazetteer of Dong’e County, Daoguang edition (Daoguang Dong’e xianzhi [道光]東阿縣志) reads:
div.quote: The huge rock at the summit is several tens of zhang high and more than one li broad. Because its shape is upright and square, like a seal with a knob, it is named ‘Seal Peak’ 印峰, and also ‘Heavenly Column’ 天柱. 1: 138. The Republican edition of the Gazetteer of Dong’e County 81, concurs.

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: See the relevant introduction to Chongfan Monastery 崇梵寺 on Mount Tianchi 天池山.

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: The Buddha name at Mount Yuncui was carved by a group of five monks. At least one of these donors, a certain bhikṣu Sengtai 僧太, is also known from the colophon carved at Mount Ergu 二鼓山 (EG 2). A second donor, whose name is damaged, is probably identical with bhikṣu Daoyong 道顒, known from the colophon at Mount Ergu.

div.head: Dating
div.p: Based on the style of the calligraphy, the inscriptions are generally dated to the Northern Qi dynasty (550-574). Those authors who connect the inscriptions to the monk Seng’an Daoyi 僧安道壹 (fl. 562-579) assign them to his early period, and date them to around 564. 51; 107.

div.head: Research History
div.p: Research on Mount Yuncui is primarily found in the Research on the Sutras Engraved on Polished Cliffs of the Northern Dynasties, vol. 2 (Beichao moya kejing yanjiu (xu) 北朝摩崖刻經研究 (續)). In this volume from 2003, the site is mentioned several times. 4, and 33; 51, 74, and 78; 104, 107, 120, and 126; 180, and 188. In the third volume of Research on the Sutras Engraved on Polished Cliffs of the Northern Dynasties from 2006, Yang Hao 楊浩 and Hao Li 郝莉 give another short description of the site. 174.

figure.head: “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” inscription, photograph taken in 2006
石刻“大空王佛”，2006年照片

div.head: Inventory
div.p: One Buddha name (YC 1) and one historical inscription (YC 2).

div.head: YC 1
div.p: One single Buddha name was carved on the western cliff of Heavenly Column Peak at Mount Yuncui, reading “Buddha King of Great Emptiness”. Immediately to the right of the inscription, and partly overlapping the characters, the contours of a round headed stele were incised at an unknown later date, but neither décor nor characters were added (fig. and fig. ).

For the meaning of the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” see the relevant introduction on Mount Hongding’s 洪頂山 Buddha names. For all occurrences of this name in Shandong Province see HDS 8, discussion.

div.head: YC 2
div.p: To the right of the incised stele next to the Buddha name, a colophon of at least five columns was carved onto the cliff face in an oblong field, framed on three sides by protruding edges. Each column contains the name of a monk, following the denomination bhikṣu (fig. ).

Since all persons mentioned in the colophon are termed bhikṣu , the Buddha name was probably carved by an association of monks. Lai Fei 賴非 believes that they must have come from the nearby Chongfan Monastery 崇梵寺, 107. and he agrees with Kiriya Seiichi 桐谷征一, who links this association to the early works of Seng’an Daoyi, known as a donor and calligrapher of stone inscriptions at Mount Hongding洪頂山, Mount Tie 鐵山, and Mount Jian 尖山. 126; 74-75.

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p: The technique is mainly “凵”-shaped carving and “Flying white” carving.

div.head: Style of Calligraphy
div.p: For YC 1 see the relevant introduction to Mount Tianchi 天池山 (TC 1). The calligraphic strokes of YC 2 are unusually angular and thin.

figure.head: Detail of carved monks’ names (YC 2); photograph taken in 2004
僧人題名局部（雲翠山2號），2004年照片
